# 'Ambitious without being arrogant': U of A professor wins Turing Prize for groundbreaking AI work

Author of the article:

By [Ramin Ostad](https://edmontonjournal.com/author/rostadpostmedia-com/)

Published Mar 07, 2025

Last updated Mar 07, 2025

2 minute read

Join the conversation

You can save this article by registering for free here . Or sign-in if you have an account.

A University of Alberta professor, Richard Sutter, who literally wrote has received the Turing Award, the most prestigious prize in computing science. Photo by Shaughn Butts / 10107376A

Article content

A University of Alberta professor who literally wrote the book on training AI software has received the most prestigious prize in computing science.

Article content

University of Alberta computing science professor Richard Sutton was honoured at a press conference Thursday at the Alberta Machine Intelligence Institute (AMII), near 100 Street and Jasper Avenue, one day after being named co-recipient of the 2024 Association for Computing Machinery A.M. Turing Award.

Article content

Article content

Article content

The award, named for British mathematician Alan M. Turing, is considered to be the “Nobel Prize for computing science” and comes with a $1-million prize funded by Google. Sutton shares the award with his longtime collaborator, Andrew Barto.

Article content

Headline News

Article content

University of Alberta president Bill Flanagan said he was “over the moon when I heard this news.”

Article content

“It’s wonderful recognition for the recognition for you, and for your groundbreaking work that has changed the world and will continue to change the world,” Flanagan told Sutton.

Article content

### Learning from experience

Article content

In the simplest terms, reinforcement learning is a technique that trains AI software to use trial-and-error decision-making to achieve the most optimal results. This is different from large language models like ChatGPT or China’s DeepSeek-R1, which learns through analyzing mass quantities of data, though they do use reinforcement learning tools to refine outcomes.

Article content

Influenced by the ideas of late AI pioneer A. Harry Klopf, Sutton and Barto began exploring the concept of an adaptive learning system that changes its behaviour to maximize a desired outcome.

Article content

Article content

“We wanted to be ambitious without being arrogant,” Sutton said. “Now it’s obvious that reinforcement learning is a thing to do, but it did not used to exist as a machine-learning endeavour.”

Article content

   Story continues below
   
   This advertisement has not loaded yet, but your article continues below.

Article content

Sutton spoke about the award’s namesake, Turing, who once said, “What we want is a machine that learns from experience.” Despite all the progress made in the field, Sutton said, they still have not achieved that dream.

Article content

He believes the future of AI won’t be in large language models but in systems that focus on reinforcement learning.

Article content

“Still today, I’d say the importance of learning from experience is not appreciated,” Sutton said. “I’ve had to explain to so many people that learning from experience is not what modern AI does. It learns from people, and it tries to mimic people.”

Article content

Sutton helped found AMII during his 25-year tenure at the University of Alberta and helped it become a world-renowned artificial intelligence hub. He says the University of Alberta is and will continue to be a key player in the future development of AI.

Article content

“The University of Alberta, far more than any other place, is the leader in reinforcement learning. It seems almost inevitable that a lot of responsibility falls to us. We’re the ones to do it or fail to do it.”

Article content
